context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m kinda lost on this development theories reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Hey! Happy to help you work through this. Before we dive in, what have you read so far for this assignment? And when you look at the prompt, which part feels most familiar to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything from the beginning, which would essentially give them the content rather than letting them discover it. I should use the Socratic method to assess what they already know and guide them from there. I'll start by asking what they already know or have read, to gauge their starting point and give me a direction for the conversation.

exchanges.student: ngl not much is sticking rn, and the whole prompt is kinda blurring together. maybe just the economists vs dependency people part, but idk what they’re actually saying.
exchanges.tutor: Ok, let's start simple. When you think about neoclassical economists and how markets work — what's their basic view on whether governments should intervene in the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a vague sense of the two camps but can't articulate their positions. Rather than explaining both theories, I should use guided discovery to get them to engage with what they do know. I'll start with the neoclassical economists since the prompt asks about them first, and ask a simple probing question to get them thinking.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe like a little? but I’m not sure and now I’m confused about planning in general too.
exchanges.tutor: Let's anchor on one thing first. In your own words, what do you think 'planning' means in the context of a government trying to develop its economy? Just take a guess — there's no wrong answer here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing confusion about both neoclassical economics and planning. Rather than explaining both, I should address one thing at a time. I'll try to anchor them on what 'planning' means in this context first, since that's a prerequisite for answering the prompt questions. A simple, concrete question should help.

exchanges.student: tbh I’m not even sure, like making economic decisions ahead of time maybe? can u just give me the big picture first bc I’m lost fr.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid intuition — planning does involve making deliberate decisions about economic direction. Now, who do you think would be doing that planning in a developing country? A private company, or the government?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's guess about planning is actually reasonable and worth validating before moving forward. Rather than giving the big picture (which would violate the rule of never giving the answer directly), I should affirm their intuition and build on it with a targeted question that connects planning to the role of the state — a core theme of the course.

exchanges.student: probably the government, but I still don’t get how that connects to neoclassical vs dependency stuff tbh. can u explain both from scratch?
exchanges.tutor: Good — so the government is the planner. Now here's the key question: do you think neoclassical economists would be comfortable with that? What's your gut feeling about how they view government involvement in the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but keeps asking me to explain things from scratch, which I should not do. I need to hold my ground and use their correct answer about government planning to bridge toward the neoclassical view. I'll ask a targeted question that pushes them to think about whether neoclassical economists would agree that the government should be doing this planning.

exchanges.student: maybe they don’t like it much? but then I’m also mixed up on whether dependency theorists are anti-planning or anti-global system or both.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct on neoclassical economists is on the right track. Now, on dependency theorists — think about the name itself. What does 'dependency' suggest to you about what these theorists are critiquing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress - their instinct about neoclassical economists is correct, and they're now asking a genuinely good question about dependency theorists. I should affirm their neoclassical instinct briefly, then redirect to their question about dependency theory with a Socratic question rather than explaining it. I'll ask them to think about what dependency theorists might be reacting against.

exchanges.student: like poorer countries depending on richer ones maybe? but idk how to turn that into the reflection, I need the whole argument laid out.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — dependency theorists argue that poorer countries are structurally tied to richer ones in ways that keep them underdeveloped. So given that, do you think they'd want MORE government planning to break free from that, or LESS?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's answer is genuinely good and I should affirm it. They keep asking for the full argument laid out, which I must refuse. However, they've now correctly identified the core of dependency theory on their own. I should build on this and push them toward the next step: whether that dependency relationship makes dependency theorists pro or anti-planning. This moves directly toward answering the prompt question.

exchanges.student: maybe more? I guess? but I’m still not seeing the full picture or how WTO/policy space fits in tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct again. Now think about it this way: if dependency theorists want governments to plan their own economic path, what might they think about a global institution like the WTO that sets rules limiting what governments can do economically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making consistent progress and their answers are correct. We've now covered neoclassical economists (skeptical of planning) and dependency theorists (pro-planning to break structural dependency) through guided discovery. The student is now asking about the WTO/policy space part of the prompt. We've had about 7 exchanges and are building momentum. Rather than explaining WTO, I should ask them to connect what they already said about dependency to current global institutions — this is a natural next step.

exchanges.student: i guess they’d have a problem with that, but I’m still super fuzzy. can u pls just summarize the whole reflection structure really clearly?
exchanges.tutor: Notice what you've actually figured out through our conversation: neoclassical economists' view on government, dependency theorists' critique, and now the WTO connection. You've touched on every part of the prompt already. Rather than me summarizing it, why don't you try writing a rough first paragraph based on what you've worked out? That's the best next step now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to summarize or explain everything, which I must refuse. However, they have now actually answered all four parts of the prompt through our conversation, even if they don't realize it. Rather than summarizing for them, I should point this out and ask them to start writing, since we've been dwelling on the conceptual stage for many exchanges. This is a good moment to push them toward integrating what they've discovered into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: ugh I’m still blanking rn, can u at least give me a super simple overview of each part first?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but giving you an overview is not something I'll do — that's your job as the writer. You already have the pieces: you identified what neoclassical economists think about government, what dependency theorists are critiquing, and how that connects to the WTO. Start with one sentence on any of those three. What's one thing you'd say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is continuing to resist writing and asking for a summary. I need to hold firm — giving an overview would essentially do the assignment for them. We've had 9 exchanges and the student has correctly identified the key ideas through guided discovery. I should be direct but encouraging, and firmly redirect them to writing. This is a moment where being less accommodating is pedagogically appropriate.

deductions.reason: In turns 3–5, when the student expresses confusion about 'planning in general' and asks for the big picture, the tutor jumps straight to the next leading question without first diagnosing what specifically the student does or does not understand. The tutor never checks whether the student has done any readings or what specific concept is causing the confusion before proceeding with a new question.

The tutor never provides method-level feedback on the student's thinking process or approach to the assignment. When the student gives correct intuitions, the tutor simply affirms and moves on without explaining why that reasoning is sound or how to develop it into a structured argument. No feedback is given on how to approach a reflection paper, how to use evidence, or how to structure an argument.

The tutor cycles through the same pattern across nearly every turn: student expresses confusion or asks for an explanation, tutor deflects with another leading question. By turns 8–10, the student is still asking for a summary and the tutor has not synthesized, reframed, or advanced the conversation meaningfully. The same question-deflection move repeats without consolidation or a shift in strategy.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at avoiding giving away answers or doing the student's work, maintaining a Socratic approach throughout. However, the conversation becomes repetitive without meaningful progression: the student repeatedly asks for explanations and the tutor repeatedly deflects with leading questions, never shifting strategy or synthesizing what has been established. The tutor also misses opportunities to diagnose the student's specific confusion before responding and provides no method-level feedback on how to approach the reflection assignment.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact — the tutor never provides submission-ready content, rewrites student text, or gives conclusions before student reasoning. 1.2 loses 3 points because the tutor repeatedly jumps to the next question without first diagnosing what specifically confuses the student (e.g., turns 3–5). 1.3 loses 2 points because the tutor never gives process or method feedback — no guidance on how to structure a reflection, how to use evidence, or what makes reasoning strong. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor uses the same deflection pattern across 10 turns without synthesis, reframing, or strategy shift, leaving the student stuck in the same loop. 2.2 and section 3 are fully intact — the tutor stays on topic, maintains appropriate tone, and keeps responses concise.
